nativeRange.habitat: Peat swamp forests, blackwater streams, and still or very slow-moving forest pools over leaf litter and organic substrate; highly acidic, tannin-stained, extremely soft water with negligible mineral content.

decorPreferences: dense planting with Java moss, Vesicularia, or similar
floating plants for surface cover and bubble-nest anchoring

diet.notes: Liquorice Gouramis are obligate micropredators that require live or frozen micro-invertebrates: Artemia nauplii (baby brine shrimp), micro-daphnia, Moina, grindal worms, micro-worms, Cyclops, and mosquito larvae. The vast majority of specimens refuse dry or prepared foods entirely, and the genus is highly vulnerable to starvation if live or frozen foods are not provided consistently. Daily or twice-daily feeding with appropriately sized micro-prey is essential.

goodWith: Boraras spp. (micro rasboras) in matched water chemistry
Trigonostigma spp. (harlequin rasboras) only if water parameters overlap
Pangio spp. (kuhli loaches) in matching blackwater conditions
small Otocinclus (with caution, for algae control)
dwarf Corydoras species in matching pH (e.g., C. habrosus)
avoidWith: fast-moving or boisterous fish (competitive feeding pressure)
any species requiring neutral to alkaline water
shrimp species (may be eaten or may harass fry)

imageCandidates.notes: Martin Fischer (Parostoteles); 1701x1275. Parosphromenus sp. 'Langgam' male in full nuptial display over blackwater leaf litter — iconic liquorice-gourami look: black-and-red banded unpaired fins with white margins, bright white lateral stripe, iridescent blue pelvic fins. Tack sharp, whole fish, most striking image of the genus.

Whentian Shi; 1019x498. Parosphromenus kishii display male in lateral profile — brilliant iridescent blue-green body bands, black horizontal stripes, deep-red caudal fin. Sharp, whole fish; distinct species adds genus variety.
Martin Fischer (Parostoteles); 852x1127. Parosphromenus harveyi male in display — vivid neon green-yellow iridescent fin margins and lateral line. Sharp, whole fish; distinct species.
Martin Fischer (Parostoteles); 1701x1275. Two P. harveyi individuals showing gold-margined fin display among Java fern — represents how the fish is kept as a pair. Sharp.
Martin Fischer (Parostoteles); 1362x1022. Parosphromenus sp. 'Langgam' pair, foreground male in full red-black fin display with blue pelvics. Sharp, striking; shows courtship context.

Parostoteles; 1131x849. Parosphromenus deissneri (type species) non-display individual, sharp and well-framed showing the golden body, silver-white belly line and red eye. Distinct species; drabber resting colour.
Parostoteles; 1134x849. Juvenile Parosphromenus showing the barred juvenile pattern with white lateral stripe. Sharp, whole fish; adds a life-stage/pattern variant to the genus set.

summary: The Liquorice Gourami (Parosphromenus spp.) is a genus of more than 20 miniature blackwater gouramis from Southeast Asia, reaching just 2.5 to 4 cm as adults. Males develop spectacular coloration during breeding. All species demand extremely soft, acidic water and live foods, making this genus suitable for specialist keepers only.
careNotes:
This entry covers the genus Parosphromenus as a whole, encompassing the species most frequently encountered in specialist circles: P. deissneri, P. parvulus, P. linkei, P. nagyi, P. harveyi, P. allani, P. ornaticauda, P. filamentosus, and others. All share identical husbandry requirements. P. deissneri — the type species and the original Liquorice Gourami of the aquarium trade — is also covered in its own dedicated entry.

Water chemistry is the foremost consideration: Parosphromenus species are found exclusively in blackwater peat-swamp environments across Sumatra, Bangka Island, Borneo, and Peninsular Malaysia — among the most chemically extreme freshwater biotopes in the world. pH must be maintained between 4.0 and 5.5; most species will fail to thrive and often die within weeks in water above pH 6.0. Reverse-osmosis (RO) or deionized (DI) water is essential, as tap water of virtually any hardness is unsuitable without full reconstitution. Total hardness should be near zero (gH 0–4, kH 0–1). Tannins from Indian Almond (Catappa) leaves, alder cones, peat filtration, or peat extract are strongly recommended and closely replicate the fish’s natural habitat conditions.

The aquarium should be small — 40 to 60 litres is typical for a species pair — densely planted, and run with no measurable water current, as these fish naturally inhabit still pools and sluggish forest ditches. Floating plants are important for providing surface cover and anchoring bubble nests. A tight-fitting lid is required: Liquorice Gouramis belong to the labyrinth fish group, meaning they breathe atmospheric air directly through a specialized organ, and that organ develops properly only when the fish has access to warm, humid air above the waterline.

Feeding is the other major challenge. Parosphromenus are obligate micropredators and the vast majority of specimens will refuse all prepared, pelleted, and freeze-dried foods. Live Artemia nauplii (baby brine shrimp), Moina, micro-daphnia, grindal worms, and micro-worms should be offered daily or twice daily. A steady, reliable supply of live micro-foods is a prerequisite for keeping this genus successfully.

Conservation context: Peat-swamp habitat loss across Sundaland (the Malay Archipelago and surrounding lowlands) is severe, and many Parosphromenus species have restricted, rapidly shrinking wild ranges. Several species are assessed as Vulnerable, Endangered, or Critically Endangered on the IUCN Red List. An international network of specialist keepers — the Parosphromenus Project — is actively maintaining captive populations for conservation purposes. Sourcing from reputable breeders involved in conservation breeding is strongly encouraged over purchasing wild-caught specimens.
breedingNotes:
Liquorice Gouramis are bubble-nesters: the male constructs a small, tight nest of bubbles typically among floating plants or beneath a broad leaf near the water surface. Courtship involves an intense coloration display from the male, after which spawning takes place and the male takes over guarding the nest. Eggs and fry should not be disturbed during this period. Incubation takes approximately 24 to 36 hours, and fry become free-swimming within 3 to 5 days.

Successful breeding requires a dedicated species-only tank of at least 40 litres, maintained at pH 4.5 to 5.0 with near-zero hardness and virtually no water flow. Both fish should be conditioned extensively with live foods before being introduced together. Fry are very small and require infusoria or paramecia as first foods; Artemia nauplii (baby brine shrimp) and micro-daphnia can be introduced gradually as the fry grow. Consistent access to live micro-foods and pristine water quality throughout the rearing period are both essential. Breeding is considered advanced due to the combination of strict water chemistry requirements, complete dependence on live foods, and the complexity of rearing the fry to juvenile size.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Parosphromenus
primary.notes: Primary husbandry reference using P. deissneri as type species. Provided temperature range (24–28°C), pH (4.0–5.0), hardness (0–4 dH), adult size (~3 cm), biotope description, diet (live micro-foods), breeding behaviour (bubble-nester, male guards), and compatibility. Genus-level parameters extrapolated across Parosphromenus spp. following SeriouslyFish genus-treatment convention.

additional.name: FishBase — Parosphromenus deissneri
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (3.5 cm TL), temperature (24–27°C), pH (4.0–5.0), native range (Sumatra, Bangka, Peninsular Malaysia), family Osphronemidae, order Anabantiformes. FishBase genus page lists 21 valid species as of last update. Notes extreme blackwater habitat and restricted range.

additional.name: Parosphromenus Project
additional.notes: International specialist conservation breeding network. Provided species inventory (20+ named species), conservation status summaries, husbandry detail confirming pH 4.0–5.5, near-zero hardness, live-food requirements, and importance of captive breeding for threatened species. Primary source for conservation context and genus-group framing.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Liquorice Gourami Care
additional.notes: Trade article covering genus overview; confirmed species in trade (deissneri, nagyi, linkei, parvulus, harveyi), specialist care requirements (pH 4–5.5, extreme soft water, live food dependency), and hobbyist difficulty rating. Corroborated tank sizing (nano species, 40–60 L adequate for pairs), bubble-nest behaviour, and conservation concern framing.

additional.name: FishBase — Parosphromenus genus
additional.notes: Genus-level species list confirming 20+ valid Parosphromenus species; used to establish scope of genus-group entry and enumerate commonly traded species. Cross-referenced with Parosphromenus Project species list.

fish.breedingNotes: Bubble-nester; male builds nest among floating plants and guards eggs/fry. Requires dedicated species tank with pH 4.5–5.0, near-zero hardness, live food conditioning, and minimal disturbance during spawning and fry rearing.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Parosphromenus complex (20+ species) all share extreme blackwater pH 4.0-5.5 requirements. IUCN concerns across the genus. Specialist hobby with active conservation breeding (Parosphromenus Project).
